event_blocks.summary: An upbeat electronic track heralded the start of OpenAI Dev Day as a female voice welcomed the audience and introduced Sam Altman. Altman, dressed in a dark green long-sleeve sweater and dark pants, walked onto a dimly lit stage holding a small black device, met by loud applause and cheers from the audience. The stage screens initially displayed 'OPENAI DEV DAY' on a light pink background. Altman greeted the audience with a clear, confident voice, expressing excitement for the event and the 'awesome' energy, prompting further applause, and welcomed them to San Francisco. He announced 'some great stuff to announce today,' then recounted the past year, mentioning the 'low-key research preview' launch of ChatGPT on November 30th of the previous year, which 'went pretty well,' eliciting chuckles. As he spoke, the background screens transitioned to a dark, pixelated pattern displaying text about ChatGPT's viral success and a comparison of '⚡ GPT-3.5' and '✨ GPT-4'. Altman then announced GPT-4 as "the most capable model out in the world," with the large screen behind him showing smartphone interfaces of a voice assistant and a ChatGPT conversation, drawing another round of applause. He explained that recent months saw the launch of "voice and vision capabilities" allowing ChatGPT to "see, hear, and speak," gesturing towards the screen, and humorously interjected, "There's a lot, you don't have to clap each time," after the audience applauded again. The screen then showed a DALL-E interface demonstrating a user requesting an image of a seal juggling, as Altman announced the launch of DALL-E 3, "the world's most advanced image model," usable "inside of ChatGPT." Moving to a wooden podium with a closed MacBook, he introduced "ChatGPT Enterprise" for business customers, detailing its "enterprise-grade security and privacy, higher speed GPT-4 access, longer context windows, lot more," while the screen displayed the 'ChatGPT Enterprise' title and logos of companies like AMGEN and Lowe’s. Gesturing, Altman highlighted statistics on the screen: "2 million developers" building on their API, "over 92% of Fortune 500 companies" using their products, and "100 million weekly active users," each presented in distinct colored boxes under warm stage lighting.

Stage Setup.description: A large stage with a dark backdrop and a wooden podium on the left, featuring a closed MacBook; the screen displays evolving presentation content with clear, bold text and graphics, illuminated by focused stage lighting.

Audience.description: A large, shadowed crowd seated in the auditorium, some holding up phones to record the event. They are highly engaged, expressing their enthusiasm through frequent, loud applause and laughter in response to the Speaker's announcements and humorous remarks.

Presentation Slides.description: Dynamic on-screen content, confirmed by the Speaker's narration, including side-by-side smartphone interfaces demonstrating a voice assistant and ChatGPT conversation, followed by a DALL-E image generation demo (showing a seal juggling), then 'ChatGPT Enterprise' with logos of major companies, and finally three colored boxes displaying '2M Developers', '92% Fortune 500', and '100M Weekly active users', all serving to visually reinforce the Speaker's announcements.

Speaker.description: A man in a dark green long-sleeve sweater and dark pants, holding a small black device, speaking confidently on stage with expressive hand gestures and direct eye contact. His voice is clear and engaging, announcing key product launches like GPT-4, voice and vision capabilities, DALL-E 3, and ChatGPT Enterprise, while also humorously interacting with the applauding audience.

He then asserted that 'numbers never tell the whole picture,' highlighting the importance of product usage, before introducing a video. event_blocks.summary: Andelyn, a student and mother of four, described ChatGPT as a "wonderful thing" that provided her "life back" and "time for my family and time for me" due to its knowledge and speed. Artist Don, who experiences chronic nerve pain limiting his left hand, found AI's voice input and dialogue the "maximum best interface." On a large stage, a Speaker in a dark green sweater announced "It's here" before revealing "GPT-4 Turbo," which he explained addresses user requests with six key improvements, starting with "context length," expanding from 8K/32K to 128,000 tokens, equivalent to "300 pages of a standard book," and is "much more accurate." He then introduced "More control" for developers, including "JSON mode" for valid responses and improved "function calling" to execute multiple actions at once. The Speaker also announced "reproducible outputs" for consistent model behavior and the ability to "view log probs in the API" soon. Under "better world knowledge," he stated GPT-4 Turbo's knowledge cutoff is updated from September 2021 to "April 2023." Finally, under "New modalities," he revealed DALL·E 3, GPT-4 Turbo with Vision, and a new text-to-speech model are available in the API, noting DALL·E 3 is used in a Coca-Cola Diwali campaign and GPT-4 Turbo with Vision can now accept images for captioning, classification, and analysis, demonstrated by an AI analyzing Barilla pasta and assisting users with the "Be My Eyes" application.

The video opens with a confident Speaker, a man in a dark green long-sleeve sweater and dark pants, standing on a large stage in front of a dark backdrop. He holds a small black device and gestures with his left hand as he announces, "On to the new stuff and we have got a lot." Behind him, the screen displays 'On to the new stuff' in bold white letters. The audience, visible in shadowed rows with some holding up phones, erupts in enthusiastic applause and cheers. The Speaker continues, detailing plans to discuss improvements and future directions, mentioning extensive conversations with developers and incorporating their feedback. As he moves across the stage, the screen transitions to a pixelated world map. He then declares, "Today, we are launching a new model. GPT-4 Turbo," prompting another wave of loud applause and cheers from the audience as the screen changes to 'GPT-4' and then 'GPT-4 Turbo'. The Speaker explains that "GPT-4 Turbo will address many of the things that you all have asked for," and introduces six major topics. The screen then displays a grid of six colored squares labeled 01 through 06. He focuses on the top-left square, stating, "Number one, context length," as 'Context length' appears under square 01. He continues to explain with expressive hand movements that many tasks require a longer context length, noting that "GPT-4 supported up to 8K and in some cases up to 32K," all while the professional stage lighting remains focused on him.

A man in a dark green long-sleeve sweater and dark pants, the Speaker, stands confidently on a stage, holding a small black remote and gesturing expressively. Behind him, a large screen initially shows a large 'O' before transitioning to a grid of six colored squares, with 'Context length' appearing under square 01. The Speaker begins by discussing the limitations of a "2K context length" before enthusiastically announcing that "GPT-4 Turbo supports up to 128,000 tokens of context," which is met with loud, enthusiastic applause from the shadowed audience, some of whom are recording with their phones. He elaborates that this represents "300 pages of a standard book, 16 times longer" and that the model is "much more accurate." As the screen changes to a pattern of white document icons and then back to the grid, now highlighting square 02 with the text 'More control', the Speaker transitions to "Number two: More control," emphasizing that "developers need more control over the model's responses." The presentation then shifts to a JSON code block showing weather data with 'JSON Mode: ON' toggled, as the Speaker introduces "JSON mode" as a "huge developer request" for ensuring valid JSON responses. Finally, a function calling demonstration appears, showing a user's request 'raise my windows and turn the radio on' processed more efficiently, with the Speaker explaining the model's improved ability to call "many functions at once" and follow instructions. The focused lighting maintains a professional and dynamic atmosphere throughout.

The segment opens with a close-up of the Speaker, a man in a dark green long-sleeve sweater and dark pants, holding a small black device and gesturing as he announces, "We're also introducing a new feature called reproducible outputs," explaining that it allows for consistent outputs and a "higher degree of control over model behavior," rolling out "in beta today." The screen behind him, initially displaying 'what about the radio?' and 'radio_on', transitions to three vertical panels of code-like data logs. The camera pulls back to reveal the full stage setup with a wooden podium and dark backdrop. The Speaker continues, mentioning that "in the coming weeks, we'll roll out a feature to let you view log probs in the API." The audience responds with applause and cheers, some holding up phones to record. The view zooms in on the Speaker, now standing beside a large number '4' on the screen, as he states, "All right, number three, better world knowledge." The presentation shifts to a grid of six colored squares (01-06, with 'Context length' under 01 and 'More control' under 02) as the Speaker walks across the stage, engaging the audience, explaining, "You want these models to be able to access better knowledge about the world. So do we." He then announces, "So we're launching retrieval in the platform," allowing users to "bring knowledge from outside documents or databases into whatever you're building." The screen changes to a network diagram, then a simple text slide reading 'September 2021'. The Speaker, gesturing expressively, addresses the "knowledge cutoff," stating, "We are just as annoyed as all of you, probably more, that GBT4's knowledge about the world ended in 2021. We will try to never let it get that out of date again." The screen updates to 'April 2023' as he concludes, "GBT4 Turbo has knowledge about the world up to April of 2023." The audience is visible in shadowed silhouettes, still engaged.

The Speaker, a man in a dark green long-sleeve sweater and dark pants, stands confidently on a large stage in front of a dark backdrop, holding a small black device and gesturing expressively. He states they will "continue to improve that over time" before announcing "Number four. New modalities." The screen behind him initially displayed a grid of six colored squares labeled 01 through 06, with text like 'Context length' and 'More control', then transitioned to a JSON code block and a 'Function calling' diagram. The Speaker then announces, "Surprising no one, DALL·E 3, GPT-4 Turbo with Vision, and the new text to speech model are all going into the API today," which is met with enthusiastic applause from the shadowed audience, some of whom are recording with their phones. As the Speaker continues to gesture, the screen transitions to showcase 'DALL·E 3', 'GPT-4 Turbo with Vision', and 'TTS' in bold white text. He explains, "We have a handful of customers that have just started using DALL·E 3 to programmatically generate images and designs," and highlights that "Today, Coke is launching a campaign that lets its customers generate Diwali cards using DALL·E 3," while a Coca-Cola Diwali-themed advertisement with a large can surrounded by candles on a beach at dusk is displayed. He adds that "safety systems help developers protect their applications against misuse," with these tools available in the API. The Speaker then shifts focus, stating, "GPT-4 Turbo can now accept images as inputs via the API, can generate captions, classifications, and analysis." He demonstrates this with a smartphone interface analyzing a box of Barilla Penne Rigate pasta, where the AI provides detailed descriptions including cooking time and weight, followed by a sequence of five smartphone screens with loading animations, indicating a multi-step process. He concludes by mentioning, "For example, Be My Eyes uses this technology to help people who are blind or have low vision with their daily tasks like..." The lighting remains focused on the speaker and screen, maintaining a professional and engaging atmosphere throughout his presentation, with a wooden podium and closed MacBook visible on the left of the stage.

event_blocks.summary: The Speaker, dressed in a dark green long-sleeve sweater, stood center stage before a large screen displaying smartphone mockups and later a grid of colored squares. He introduced a new text-to-speech model generating natural audio, demonstrated by a female voice narrating a historical fact, and highlighted its benefits for accessibility and use cases like language learning. The screen then showed 'Whisper V3' as he announced the release of the open-source speech recognition model, emphasizing improved multilingual performance. Transitioning to features, the screen displayed '16K' as he announced the expansion of fine-tuning to the 16K version of GPT-3.5 and invited users to apply for GPT-4 fine-tuning. He then introduced 'Custom Models,' explaining that researchers would collaborate with companies to create custom models, including domain-specific pre-training and custom RL post-training. The Speaker also announced doubling tokens per minute for GPT-4 customers, showing an interface with a rate limit of 20,000 RPM for gpt-3.5-turbo, and stated users could request further rate limit changes. Finally, he introduced 'Copyright Shield,' explaining the company would defend customers against copyright infringement claims, applicable to both ChatGPT Enterprise and the API, concluding with a slide stating, 'We do not train on your data from the API, or ChatGPT Enterprise, ever.' Throughout the presentation, the audience, visible as shadowed silhouettes, recorded with phones, while the stage setup remained consistent with a wooden podium and MacBook.

event_blocks.summary: The Speaker, dressed in a dark green sweater, stood on a dimly lit stage with a shadowed audience, some recording with phones, to discuss the future of AI. He introduced the concept of AI agents, emphasizing OpenAI's belief in gradual iterative deployment to address safety concerns and the need for societal consideration. The screen behind him transitioned from a black backdrop to display the 'ChatGPT' logo and a grid of app icons, illustrating AI applications. He then announced the introduction of 'GPTs,' defined as tailored versions of ChatGPT for specific purposes, which can be built with instructions, expanded knowledge, and actions, making them more helpful and controllable. The screen visually represented these components with colored panels. The Speaker highlighted the accessibility of building GPTs through natural language programming, giving agency to everyone. He then showcased examples of GPTs, including the 'Code.org Lesson Planner,' which helps teachers explain complex concepts like four loops using analogies, and the 'Canva GPT,' enabling users to design by describing their needs in natural language, as demonstrated by the generation of DevDay reception posters. The presentation aimed to cover what GPTs are, how to use and build them, and their distribution and discovery.

event_blocks.summary: Jessica, a solutions architect with long black hair and black-framed glasses, wearing a light pink blazer, took the stage to enthusiastic applause to demonstrate Zapier's GPT capabilities. She initiated the demo by interacting with her calendar, typing "What's on my schedule for today?" into the chat interface, emphasizing that the system would always ask for permission before taking action or sharing data. After approving the request, the screen displayed her schedule, including a noted conflict, illustrating how GPT could identify issues. Jessica then demonstrated how to notify a colleague, Sam, about leaving early by typing, "let Sam know, I gotta go. Um, chasing GPUs," which resulted in a Slack message to 'sama'. Upon confirmation from Sam that he received the message, Jessica expressed her excitement for what users would build and handed the stage back to Sam. Sam, in a dark green sweater, returned to the stage, acknowledging Jessica's examples and mentioning the growing number of GPTs being created. He then introduced the theme 'Build with natural language,' explaining that coding knowledge wouldn't be necessary as users could program GPTs through conversation. Sam proceeded to build a new GPT live, intending to create one that advises startup founders and challenges them on growth. The system suggested the name 'Startup Mentor,' generated a profile picture, and outlined sample prompts. Sam then uploaded a lecture transcript, 'Lecture 2 Ideas Products Teams and Execution Part II.txt,' to provide the GPT with specific knowledge, configuring it to be concise and constructive. He enabled 'Web Browsing' and 'Code Interpreter,' and demonstrated a query about hiring employees, to which 'Startup Mentor' responded with key attributes: 'Smart,' 'Gets Things Done,' and 'Cultural Fit.' Sam noted that the GPT would utilize the uploaded document and GPT-4's knowledge. He then published the GPT privately, mentioning the possibility of future public sharing. Concluding the GPT creation demonstration, Sam announced the upcoming launch of the GPT store later that month, where users could list their GPTs, with the best ones being featured. He assured the audience that GPTs in the store would adhere to policies and highlighted the importance of revenue sharing, stating that creators of the most useful and used GPTs would receive a portion of OpenAI's revenue, fostering a vibrant ecosystem.

raw_segments: The segment opens with a man, the Speaker, in a dark green sweater, standing on a large stage with a dark backdrop and wooden paneling. He holds a small black device and speaks expressively, detailing how Canva GPT can generate poster design options for a DevDay reception by hitting Canva's APIs, evolving plugins into custom actions for GPTs. Behind him, the screen displays the 'Canva GPT' interface with two poster designs, then transitions to the Canva design editor. He explains users can chat for iterations and click through to Canva for the full design experience. The Speaker then announces a live GPT demonstration, introducing Zapier's GPT, which performs actions across 6,000 applications. He introduces Jessica, a solutions architect, to drive the demo. As he welcomes her, a new person, a woman with long black hair and large black-framed glasses, walks onto the stage from the right to enthusiastic applause and cheers from the shadowed audience, some recording with phones. She approaches a wooden podium with a laptop, and the screen behind her displays the 'OpenAI DevDay' logo. Jessica, wearing a light pink blazer, thanks the previous speaker and the audience, then introduces herself as Jessica Shay, explaining her role in working with partners and customers to bring products to life, expressing her excitement to show their hard work.

The scene opens on a large auditorium stage where a woman with long black hair and large black-framed glasses, wearing a light pink blazer over a gray top, stands at a wooden podium with a laptop. She begins speaking clearly, stating, "So, let's get started." She gestures with her right hand as she explains that the GPT interface is on the upper left corner. Behind her, a massive screen displays a split view of ChatGPT and a mobile calendar app for November 6th. She proceeds to click on 'Zapier AI Actions', narrating her action, and identifies the calendar on the right as "my calendar for today." She explains that her calendar is already connected and then types "What's on my schedule for today?" into the chat box. She emphasizes the security features, stating that before any action or data sharing, the system "will ask for your permission." She then approves the permission request, saying "allowed." The screen updates to display a formatted list of her scheduled events, including a noted scheduling conflict. She elaborates on how "GPT is designed to take in your instructions, make the decision on which capability to call to perform that action, and then execute that for you," further highlighting that it "pulls into my information" and can "identify conflicts on my calendar." The engaged audience, visible as silhouettes, records the demonstration with their phones under professional stage lighting.

The video opens with Jessica, a woman with long black hair and large black-framed glasses, wearing a light pink blazer over a gray top, standing at a wooden podium on a large stage. She speaks clearly and articulately, explaining, "So, it looks like I have something coming up. So, what if I want to let Sam know that I have to leave early?" As she speaks, a large screen behind her displays a dark interface titled 'Zapier AI Actions' alongside a Google Calendar view showing scheduled events for 'DevDay' on November 6. She types into a chat interface, saying, "let Sam know, I gotta go. Um, chasing GPUs," which is then sent to actions.zapier.com and transitions to show a direct message in Slack to 'sama'. She continues, "So, with that, I'm gonna swap to my conversation with Sam and then I'm gonna say, yes, please run that," followed by typing sounds. She then asks, "Sam? Did you get that?" to which a male voice replies, "I did." Jessica exclaims, "Awesome," as the audience erupts in applause and cheers. She concludes, "So, this is only a glimpse of what is possible, and I cannot wait to see what you all will build. Thank you, and back to you, Sam," prompting more audience applause. The scene then cuts to Sam, a man in a dark green long-sleeve sweater and dark pants, walking onto the dimly lit stage from the right. He holds up a small black device, revealing a phone screen displaying the time '10:25', before lowering it and gesturing with his hands. Behind him, a black screen displays the OpenAI logo, featuring a red shield flanked by a golden lion and tiger, with a blue banner below. Sam addresses the shadowed audience, some of whom are recording, saying, "Thank thank you, Jessica. So, those are three great examples. In addition to these, there are many more kinds of GPTs that people are creating and many, many more."

The video opens with the Speaker, a man in a dark green long-sleeve sweater and dark pants, standing center stage in front of a large dark screen displaying the OpenAI logo and a crest. He speaks with a clear, articulate voice and expressive hand gestures, introducing the theme 'Build with natural language' as the text appears on screen. He explains, "We know that many people who want to build a GPT, don't know how to code. We've made it so that you can program the GPT just by having a conversation," emphasizing that "natural language is going to be a big part of how people use computers in the future." The camera pulls back to reveal the full stage setup, including a wooden panel on the right and a shadowed, attentive audience seated in the auditorium, some holding up phones to record. The Speaker walks to a wooden podium on the left, where a laptop is placed, announcing, "So I'd like to show you how to build one." He begins a live demonstration, with the screen behind him transitioning to show the OpenAI DevDay logo, then to a browser interface of the ChatGPT platform, specifically the 'My GPTs' page with options like 'Zapier AI Actions' and 'Canva GPT'. He interacts with the interface, clicking to create a new GPT, and the screen displays the 'GPT Builder' prompt: 'What would you like to make?'. He states his intention, "I want to create a GPT that helps give founders and developers advice when starting new projects," drawing on his experience working with founders. As the audience watches attentively, he types into the input field, beginning with "I want to help startup founders..." The lighting remains focused on the speaker and the screen, creating a professional and engaging atmosphere.

The video opens with the Speaker, a man in a dark green long-sleeve sweater, standing at a wooden podium on a large stage with a dark backdrop. He articulately dictates and types a detailed instruction into the ChatGPT interface displayed on a massive screen behind him, which shows the 'New GPT' creation screen with the 'GPT Builder' prompt. He instructs the system, "I want to help startup founder think through their business ideas and get advice. After the founder has gotten some advice, grill them on why they are not growing faster!" This elicits laughter and applause from the audience, visible as silhouettes in the foreground, some recording with phones. The screen then displays 'Updating GPT...' as the system processes his request. The Speaker continues to explain the process, noting how the system will "write some detailed instructions for the GPT." The interface updates to suggest the name 'Startup Mentor,' which the Speaker confirms by typing "that's good" and verbally affirming, "That's fine. Uh, that's good." A stylized profile picture of a person in a suit with a lightbulb is then generated and appears on the screen. The Preview section updates to show the new GPT's name, 'Startup Mentor,' and its description: 'I assist and challenge startup founders on growth,' along with sample prompts like 'How can I refine my business idea?' and 'Why am I not seeing faster growth?' The Speaker highlights this, explaining how the system is "already starting to fill out the GPT" and "has some like ideas of additional questions that I could ask." The stage lighting remains focused on the Speaker and the screen, maintaining a professional and engaging atmosphere.

The minute opens with the Speaker, a man in a dark green long-sleeve sweater, chuckling softly at the 'Startup Mentor' GPT's dynamically generated profile picture on the massive screen behind him. Standing at a wooden podium on a dimly lit stage, he expresses his approval, stating, "I sort of like that," before typing "that's great" into the ChatGPT interface. As the GPT is visibly built out further, the Speaker articulates his next instruction: "I am going to upload transcripts of some lectures about startups I have given. Please give advice based off of those." He types this message, and the interface instantly generates a detailed description for 'Startup Mentor,' emphasizing its role in providing thoughtful advice and challenging founders. The Speaker then navigates to the 'Configure' tab, explaining to the attentive, phone-recording audience that he wants to show them the capabilities and custom actions. He mentions he's "going to upload a file," and the screen visually confirms the upload of 'Lecture 2 Ideas Products Teams and Execution Part II.txt' from his 'Documents' folder, integrating real-world knowledge into the AI.

On a dimly lit stage, a Speaker in a dark green long-sleeve sweater stands at a wooden podium, actively demonstrating the creation of a custom GPT named 'Startup Mentor' within the ChatGPT interface displayed on a massive screen behind him. He explains, "here is a lecture that I picked that I used to that I gave with some startup advice," directly referencing the 'Lecture 2 Ideas Products T...' document uploaded under the 'Knowledge' section of the 'Configure' tab. As he configures the GPT, he adds an instruction to "be concise and constructive with feedback." The screen shows 'Web Browsing' and 'Code Interpreter' enabled, and the 'Preview' section displays the 'Startup Mentor' profile with its description: 'I assist and challenge startup founders on growth,' alongside pre-set 'Conversation starters' like 'How can I refine my business idea?'. The Speaker then announces, "we can try it out over on this preview tab," and proceeds to type a new query into the chat box, audibly asking, "What are three things to look for when hiring employees at an early stage startup?". The 'Startup Mentor' promptly responds on screen with a structured list of three key attributes: 'Smart', 'Gets Things Done', and 'Cultural Fit', each with a detailed explanation. The Speaker concludes by explaining that the system will utilize "that document I uploaded" and "all of the background knowledge of GPT-4." The audience, visible as silhouettes in the foreground, watches attentively, with some recording the engaging tech demonstration with their phones.

The video opens with the Speaker, a man in a dark green long-sleeve sweater, standing at a wooden podium on a large stage, demonstrating the ChatGPT interface. The massive screen behind him displays the 'New GPT' creation screen, where he types a prompt to help startup founders. The system responds by suggesting 'Startup Mentor' as a name, and a profile picture is generated. The Speaker then configures the GPT, adding capabilities like 'Web Browsing' and 'DALL·E Image Generation', and uploads a document titled 'Lecture 2 Ideas Products T...'. He scrolls through the preview, which outlines three key traits for hiring: Smart, Gets Things Done, and Cultural Fit, remarking in a clear voice, "That's pretty good. Those are three things that I definitely have said many times." He explains that while they could go on, he'll skip ahead in the interest of time, stating, "I'm going to publish this only to me for now," but notes he can add more content and actions later, and "then I can share it publicly." He concludes the demonstration by saying, "So that's what it looks like to create a GPT." As he steps away from the podium, holding a small black device, the audience erupts in loud applause and cheering, to which the Speaker responds, "Thank you." He then speaks to the audience, gesturing with his hands, sharing, "By the way, I always, I always wanted to do that after like all of the YC office hours, I always thought, man, someday I will be able to make a bot that will do this and that'll be awesome." The screen transitions to a black background, then displays three minimalist icons: an orange circle with a white arc, a globe with colored segments, and a grid of squares. The Speaker continues to speak center stage, with the audience visible in shadowed rows in the foreground, some holding up phones to record. He explains, "So, with GPTs, we're letting people easily share and discover all the fun ways that they use ChatGPT with the world," adding that "You can make private GPTs, like I just did," before the audio cuts off as he mentions sharing.

The segment opens with the Speaker, a man in a dark green long-sleeve sweater, standing on a large stage with a dark backdrop, gesturing expressively as he explains how users can share their "creations publicly" or make GPTs "just for your company" on ChatGPT Enterprise. Behind him, a massive screen initially displays three simple, colorful icons representing a person, a globe, and a building. He then announces, "And later this month, we're going to launch the GPT store," which is met with enthusiastic applause and cheers from the audience, visible as silhouettes in the foreground, some recording with phones. As the screen transitions to a detailed view of the 'chat.openai.com/store' page, showcasing 'Featured GPTs' like 'Reactify' and 'Cumulus,' alongside 'GPTs powered by DALL·E' with vibrant AI-generated cityscapes, the Speaker continues, "You can list a GPT there, and we'll be able to feature the best and the most popular GPTs." He assures the audience that "GPTs in the store follow our policies before they're accessible." The camera zooms in on his focused expression and animated hand movements as the screen changes to a black background with the white text 'Revenue sharing.' He emphasizes, "Revenue sharing is important to us," explaining, "We're going to pay people who build the most useful and the most used GPTs a portion of our revenue." Holding a small device, he concludes with excitement, "We're excited to foster a vibrant ecosystem with the GPT store... We're confident there's going to be a lot of great stuff."

event_blocks.summary: The Speaker, dressed in a dark green long-sleeve sweater and dark pants, walked across a large stage with a dark backdrop and wooden paneling, gesturing expressively while holding a small device. He announced, "But this is a developer conference, and the coolest thing about this is that we're bringing the same concept to the API," as the massive screen behind him initially displayed 'API' in white text. The audience, visible as silhouettes, erupted in enthusiastic applause and cheers, with some recording with phones. The Speaker explained that many have been building "agent-like experiences on the API." The screen then transitioned to show the logos of Shopify, Discord, and Snapchat, arranged horizontally, as the Speaker detailed how these companies utilize the API for 'Sidekick,' 'Clyde,' and 'My AI' respectively, describing them as customized chatbots and tools for actions and personalities. He acknowledged that while "These experiences are great," they "have been hard to build," sometimes taking "months, teams of dozens of engineers." The screen then changed to list four bullet points: 'State management', 'Prompt and context management', 'Extend capabilities', and 'Retrieval', before transitioning to the title 'Assistants API'. The Speaker announced, "So today we're making that a lot easier with our new Assistants API," which was met with another round of loud applause and cheers from the audience. Finally, the screen displayed a colorful icon labeled 'Threading', depicting interconnected blocks, as the Speaker continued his presentation with focused gestures and a calm demeanor, beginning to explain what the Assistants API includes.

The content directly supports and is referenced by the Speaker's verbal explanations of technical concepts and product features.

event_blocks.summary: On a large stage with a dark backdrop and wooden paneling, Sam, wearing a dark green sweater, introduced concepts like 'persistent threads,' 'built-in retrieval,' 'code interpreter,' and 'improved function calling,' with corresponding icons displayed on a massive screen behind him, while the audience watched as silhouettes, some recording on their phones. Sam then welcomed Ramon, head of developer experience, to the stage, exiting stage left as upbeat music played and the audience applauded. Ramon, in a dark blue shirt and light jeans, walked on from the right, waving to the cheering crowd and approaching a podium with a laptop. He thanked Sam and greeted the audience enthusiastically, announcing new API modalities and improved developer experience for building assistive agents, then transitioned the screen to a file explorer interface. Ramon explained he was building a travel app called 'Wanderlust,' showcasing its web interface with a 'Discover' tab featuring destinations like 'Hyderabad' and 'Taipei,' noting he used GPT-4 for these ideas. He further elaborated that the app's illustrations were generated programmatically using the new DALL-E 3 API. Ramon then demonstrated creating a simple assistant in the OpenAI Playground, configuring it with GPT-4 Turbo, Code Interpreter, and Retrieval tools. He transitioned to a code editor displaying TypeScript, explaining the integration of the assistant API's 'threads and messages' primitives, detailing how new threads are created for each user. The presenter then showed a close-up of TypeScript code in `useAssistant.tsx` for streaming responses, and demonstrated the 'Wanderlust' app where typing 'Let's go to Paris!' prompted the map to zoom and the assistant to provide a detailed description. He highlighted function calling's power, including guaranteed JSON output and invoking multiple functions simultaneously, and demonstrated this by asking for the 'top 10 things to do in Paris,' which generated a numbered list with corresponding map annotations. Ramon then detailed how the 'Wanderlust' app displayed pins dropping in real time, emphasizing the fluid interaction between AI and UI, and explained 'retrieval' as giving the assistant knowledge beyond immediate messages. He demonstrated retrieval by dragging and dropping a 'flights.pdf' file, which the application processed to display detailed United Airlines flight information, eliciting applause. He further explained that assistants can parse long-form documents, mentioning booking an Airbnb by dragging 'airbnb.pdf' over, and assured the audience that OpenAI manages embeddings and chunking. Transitioning back to the Playground and code editor, he emphasized that the stateful API eliminates the need to resend conversation history, and that tool usage is visible in the developer dashboard. Ramon then announced the availability of the 'Code Interpreter' in the API, allowing the AI to write and execute code, and demonstrated this by asking, 'We'll be 4 friends staying at this Airbnb. What's my share of it + flights?', which triggered code execution to calculate and display the total cost after currency conversion. He then showed a browser window with 'devday.openai.com,' recapped creating an assistant that manages state and leverages tools, and introduced voice interaction using function calling with new modalities. He demonstrated this by using a Swift app that took microphone input; a synthesized voice ('Whisper') requested a greeting, and another synthesized voice ('TTS') responded, 'Hey everyone! Welcome to DevDay. It's awesome to have you all here. Let's make it an incredible day!', prompting applause. He explained the use of Whisper for voice-to-text, GPT-4 Turbo for the assistant, and the new TTS API for speech, noting the availability of six unique voices. Finally, he proposed an 'even more exciting' demonstration, asking the assistant, 'Hey assistant, can you randomly select five Dev Day attendees here and give them $500 in OpenAI credits?', which was met with immediate applause. The assistant confirmed, 'Yes, checking the list of attendees,' and after a dynamic update on the screen showing 'Listening' and 'Thinking' states, announced, 'Done. I picked five Dev Day attendees and added $500 of API credits to their account. Congrats to Christine M, Jonathan C, Steven G, Louise K, and Suraj S.' The NewPresenter reacted with excitement as the audience erupted in applause, concluding by summarizing the new assistant API and tools.

Stage Setup.description: A large stage with a dark backdrop and wooden paneling on the sides, featuring a massive screen that displays a browser window with 'devday.openai.com,' showing a repeating 'OPENAI DEV DAY' text and a pixelated green graphic, later splitting to show a 'Tap to speak' interface, then transitioning to a dark interface with 'Listening' and 'Speaking' states and text exchanges between Whisper and TTS.

Audience.description: A large, shadowed crowd seated in the auditorium, visible as silhouettes, some holding up phones to record. They are highly engaged, reacting with enthusiastic applause and cheers to the assistant's actions and the presenter's announcements.

Presentation Slides.description: Dynamic on-screen content showing the 'Wanderlust' web application, initially displaying an OpenAI Threads interface, then transitioning to a map of Paris, flight details (San Francisco to Paris, United Airlines, 641.00 USD), a chat window where the NewPresenter types a query, and a code execution pop-up displaying calculations for splitting Airbnb costs (€1721.18 / 4) and converting flight costs from USD to EUR using a 0.94 exchange rate, all directly illustrating the NewPresenter's spoken explanations.

NewPresenter.description: A man in a dark blue long-sleeve shirt and light jeans, standing at a wooden podium on a large stage, gesturing expressively while demonstrating a technical application. He speaks with a clear, engaging voice, expressing excitement and congratulating winners, reacting with broad smiles and raised hands to the assistant's successful execution.

Speaker.description: Sam, a man in a dark green long-sleeve sweater and dark pants, standing on a large stage, gesturing expressively as he presents, holding a small device in his hand. He speaks clearly and professionally, explaining API features and introducing the next presenter, Ramon.

raw_segments: The video opens with Sam, a man in a dark green long-sleeve sweater and dark pants, standing on a large stage with a dark backdrop and wooden paneling. He gestures expressively while speaking clearly and professionally about 'persistent threads,' 'built-in retrieval,' 'code interpreter' (a working Python interpreter in a sandbox environment), and 'improved function calling,' all of which are displayed with corresponding icons on a massive screen behind him. The audience, visible as silhouettes, watches attentively, some recording with their phones. Sam then announces, "So we'd like to show you a demo of how this works, and here is Ramon, our head of developer experience. Welcome, Ramon!" As Sam exits stage left, upbeat, energetic background music plays, accompanied by audience applause. A new presenter, Ramon, a man in a dark blue long-sleeve shirt and light jeans, walks onto the stage from the right, waving to the cheering crowd. He approaches a wooden podium with a laptop, thanks Sam, and enthusiastically greets the audience with a slight accent, saying, "Good morning! Wow. It's fantastic to see you all here." Ramon expresses how inspiring it is to see AI infused into apps and announces new API modalities and improved developer experience for building assistive agents, concluding, "So let's dive right in." The screen behind him transitions to show a file explorer interface with various project files like 'useAssistant.tsx' and 'README.md'. Ramon then explains he's building a travel app called 'Wanderlust,' and the screen displays its web application interface with a 'Discover' tab, showcasing destinations like 'Hyderabad' and 'Taipei.' He gestures toward the screen, engaging the audience as he explains he used GPT-4 to come up with these destination ideas.

The segment opens with the NewPresenter, a man in a dark blue long-sleeve shirt and light jeans, standing at a wooden podium on a large stage, gesturing expressively as he explains that the illustrations in the 'Wanderlust' web application, displayed on the massive screen behind him, are generated programmatically using the new DALL-E 3 API, calling it "pretty remarkable." He then announces his intention to enhance the app by adding a "very simple assistant," switching the screen to the OpenAI Playground. He demonstrates creating an assistant, giving it a name, initial instructions, selecting GPT-4 Turbo as the model, and enabling tools like Code Interpreter and Retrieval, stating, "our assistant is ready to go." The screen then transitions to a code editor (Cursor) displaying TypeScript code, as the presenter explains integrating with "two new primitives" of the assistant API: "threads and messages." He details how for each new user, a new thread is created, and their messages are added to these threads, concluding, "Very simple." Throughout, the audience watches attentively as silhouettes, some recording on their phones, under focused lighting.

The video segment opens with a close-up of a code editor displaying TypeScript code for an OpenAI Assistant API integration within `useAssistant.tsx`, showing logic for sending messages and handling errors via a `fetchEventSource` call. The presenter, speaking with a clear, articulate voice, explains, "I can simply run the assistant at any time to stream the responses back to the app." The scene transitions to a browser window showing the OpenAI Playground, where an assistant named 'Wanderlust' is configured as a helpful travel assistant using `gpt-4-1106-preview` with Code Interpreter and Retrieval tools. The presenter then states, "we can return to the app and try that in action," as the view shifts to the 'Wanderlust' web application running locally, prompting 'Where would you like to go?' alongside a world map. The presenter types "Let's go to Paris!" into the input field, triggering the map to zoom into Paris, France. The assistant responds with a detailed description of Paris, highlighting its art, fashion, and key landmarks, then asks if the user wants information on travel, accommodation, or attractions. The presenter remarks, "With just a few lines of code, users can now have a very specialized assistant right inside the app," before introducing "one of my favorite features here: function calling," noting its power and new capabilities like guaranteed JSON output and the ability to "invoke multiple functions at once for the first time." He then types "What are the top 10 things to do in Paris?", and the assistant generates a numbered list of top attractions. As emojis representing each attraction annotate the map, the presenter emphasizes, "the assistant knows about functions, including those to annotate the map that you see on the right," gesturing towards the screen. The audience is visible in the foreground, some recording the dynamic interaction on the large screen.

The segment opens with NewPresenter, a man in a dark blue long-sleeve shirt and light jeans, confidently presenting from a wooden podium to a shadowed audience in a large auditorium. He speaks with a clear, confident voice, explaining how the 'Wanderlust' web application, displayed on the massive screen behind him, shows "all of these pins are dropping in real time here," which is met with audience applause. He continues, detailing how the natural language interface allows fluid interaction between AI and UI, where the assistant takes action. Transitioning to discussing 'retrieval,' he describes it as giving the assistant "more knowledge beyond his immediate user messages." He then demonstrates this by stating he's booked tickets to Paris and proceeds to drag and drop a 'flights.pdf' document from the Downloads folder onto the screen's file upload interface. As the application processes the file, he explains that "retrieval is reading these files," and with a "boom!", the screen dynamically updates to display detailed United Airlines flight information from San Francisco to Paris, including departure/arrival times, price, and options to 'Check In' or 'Share Itinerary,' along with a confirmation message: 'Your flight information to Paris is displayed. Have a great trip! If you need any more assistance or details, feel free to ask.' This successful demonstration elicits another round of applause from the engaged audience, some of whom are recording the event on their phones, as the NewPresenter begins to elaborate further.

A man, identified as NewPresenter, in a dark blue long-sleeve shirt and light jeans, stands at a wooden podium on a large stage, gesturing expressively as he demonstrates the 'Wanderlust' travel assistant application. The massive screen behind him displays the app's interface, showing actions like 'Read files' and 'Uploading airbnb.pdf', alongside a map of Paris and flight details. He explains in a clear, confident voice how "assistants can parse long-form documents from extensive text to intricate product specs," and mentions booking an Airbnb, stating he's "just gonna drag that over to the conversation." He addresses the audience, visible as silhouettes recording on their phones, acknowledging "how hard that is to build yourself," detailing the previous need to "compute your own embeddings" and "set up chunking algorithm," before assuring them "all of that is taken care of." The screen transitions to the OpenAI Playground, where the assistant is configured with tools like Code Interpreter and Retrieval, then to a code editor displaying TypeScript code. NewPresenter continues, explaining that "more than retrieval" is offered, and how the previous need to "resend the entire conversation history" with each API call, involving "setting up a key value store" and "handling the context windows," "completely goes away with this new stateful API." He emphasizes that "just because OpenAI is managing this API, does not mean it's a black box," assuring that "you can see the steps that the tools are taking right inside your developer dashboard," before indicating he will "go ahead and click on threads" to show the current working thread.

The segment opens with NewPresenter, a man in a dark blue long-sleeve shirt, standing at a wooden podium on a large stage, his clear, confident, slightly accented male voice explaining the steps and functions being called by the assistant. The massive screen behind him initially displays the OpenAI Threads interface, then transitions to the 'Wanderlust' web application, showing a map of Paris and flight details. NewPresenter announces a new, highly requested capability: the 'Code Interpreter' is now available in the API, giving the AI the ability to write and execute code on the fly and even generate files. As he gestures expressively, he demonstrates this by typing a query into the 'Wanderlust' chat window, narrating his input: 'We'll be 4 friends staying at this Airbnb. What's my share of it + flights?'. The screen dynamically updates, showing a pop-up window where the assistant runs code. NewPresenter explains that the code interpreter is computing the number of days and friends, and performing exchange rate calculations behind the scenes to answer the query, which includes splitting the Airbnb cost (€1721.18 / 4) and converting the flight cost from USD to EUR (using a 0.94 exchange rate). The assistant then provides the final calculated total cost for the user. Throughout, the audience is visible in the foreground as shadowed silhouettes, some recording the event on their phones, while the stage's dark backdrop and wooden paneling frame the dynamic screen content.

The video opens with a NewPresenter, a man in a dark blue long-sleeve shirt and light jeans, standing at a wooden podium on a large stage. He speaks with a clear, articulate voice, gesturing expressively as he explains how a code interpreter can handle complex tasks like crunching numbers and plotting charts, stating, "you get the picture."
